county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Adair County formed 1907 at statehood from the Cherokee Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Stilwell. Named for the Adair family, prominent in Cherokee history. Located in the Ozark foothills of northeastern Oklahoma. Cherokee heritage is predominant.
county_metadata.research_tips: Adair County was part of the Cherokee Nation in Indian Territory before statehood (1907). The Cherokee Heritage Center in Tahlequah and the Cherokee Nation Registration Department hold tribal enrollment records, Dawes Roll cards, and allotment records critical for tracing Cherokee families. The Oklahoma Historical Society (OHS) in Oklahoma City holds Indian Territory court records from the US Court for the Northern District of Indian Territory (Vinita sessions). County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907) for deeds and marriages. For pre-statehood land records, check Dawes allotment records at NARA Fort Worth and the National Archives online catalog. Cherokee census rolls (1835 Henderson Roll, 1848 Siler Roll, 1851 Chapman Roll, 1880 Census of Cherokee Nation, Dawes Rolls 1898-1914) are essential. FamilySearch has indexed many Indian Territory and Oklahoma vital records. The Stilwell Public Library has local history and cemetery transcription files.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Adair County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

source_profiles.survival_notes: County records complete from 1907; allotment records at NARA are substantially complete

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Alfalfa County formed 1907 at statehood from the Cherokee Outlet (opened by land run 1893). County seat is Cherokee. Named for William H. 'Alfalfa Bill' Murray, later governor of Oklahoma. Located in north-central Oklahoma's wheat belt.
county_metadata.research_tips: Alfalfa County was formed at statehood (1907) from the Cherokee Outlet, opened by land run on September 16, 1893. The County Clerk in Cherokee holds deeds and marriage licenses from 1893 (territorial era). For original land claims from the Cherokee Outlet run, check BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov and Oklahoma Territory land office records at NARA Fort Worth. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial census records, newspaper files, and homestead final proof documents. German-Russian (Mennonite) settlers established communities in the county -- check church records at Bethel College (North Newton, KS) and local Mennonite congregations. FamilySearch has Oklahoma Territory and state marriage records. The Alfalfa County Genealogical Society has published cemetery transcriptions and local history materials. The Cherokee (town) public library has county history resources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Alfalfa County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Atoka County formed 1907 at statehood from the Choctaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Atoka. Named for Captain Atoka, a Choctaw leader. Located in southeastern Oklahoma. Confederate forces and Choctaw soldiers operated in the area during the Civil War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Atoka County was part of the Choctaw Nation in Indian Territory. Pre-statehood records are primarily tribal: Choctaw Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls (Choctaw by Blood, Choctaw Freedmen), and tribal court records. The Choctaw Nation headquarters in Durant maintains tribal records and citizenship files. NARA Fort Worth holds Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA) Choctaw Agency records, allotment files, and land patents. The Oklahoma Historical Society (OHS) has Choctaw Nation court records, census rolls (1885 Choctaw Census), and Indian Territory newspapers. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). For Civil War era, the Choctaw Nation allied with the Confederacy -- Confederate service records for Choctaw soldiers are at NARA. Coal mining in the area produced employment records. Freedmen records (Choctaw Freedmen rolls) are essential for African American research in this county. The Atoka County Library has local genealogical materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Atoka County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.parent_county: No Man's Land / Beaver County, Oklahoma Territory
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Beaver County formed 1890 as part of Oklahoma Territory from the former 'No Man's Land' (Oklahoma Panhandle). County seat is Beaver. Originally encompassed the entire Panhandle before Cimarron and Texas counties were created. Ranching and agriculture dominate.
county_metadata.research_tips: Beaver County is in the Oklahoma Panhandle, formerly known as 'No Man's Land' or the 'Neutral Strip' -- it was not part of any state or territory from 1850 to 1890. Settlers organized a self-governing Cimarron Territory (unofficial) in 1887. The area became part of Oklahoma Territory in 1890 under the Organic Act. Records are sparse before 1890. County Clerk records begin in 1890. BLM GLO land patents are available at glorecords.blm.gov. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial-era newspapers, land office records, and census records. The Beaver County Pioneer Museum and the No Man's Land Museum in Goodwell have local historical materials. Cattle ranching was the primary industry -- brand registration records may be at the county level. The area was impacted by the Dust Bowl in the 1930s, causing significant population loss. FamilySearch has Oklahoma Territory and county marriage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Beaver County; County Clerk deed books from 1890

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Beckham County formed 1907 at statehood from Roger Mills County. County seat is Sayre. Named for J.C.W. Beckham, Governor of Kentucky. Located in western Oklahoma along the North Fork of the Red River. Route 66 is an important cultural landmark.
county_metadata.research_tips: Beckham County was created at statehood (1907) from part of Roger Mills County. Early settlement came with the Cheyenne-Arapaho opening (1892). County Clerk records in Sayre begin from the Roger Mills County territorial era. Route 66 passed through the county, bringing transient populations. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial-era newspapers and court records. BLM GLO land patents are available online. Check Roger Mills County for pre-1907 records since Beckham was carved from it. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial and state marriage records. The Shortgrass Country Museum in Sayre has local history collections. Cemetery records have been transcribed by county genealogical volunteers and are on Find A Grave.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Beckham County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Blaine County formed 1890 as original Oklahoma Territory county from former Cheyenne and Arapaho tribal lands (opened 1892). County seat is Watonga. Named for James G. Blaine. Located in west-central Oklahoma.
county_metadata.research_tips: Blaine County was one of the original Oklahoma Territory counties (1890), formed from former Cheyenne and Arapaho tribal lands opened to non-Indian settlement in 1892. County Clerk records in Watonga begin from 1892. The Cheyenne and Arapaho Tribes headquarters in Concho (near El Reno) maintain tribal records. NARA Fort Worth holds Cheyenne-Arapaho Agency records, allotment files, and annuity rolls. BLM GLO land patents are available online. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers, census records, and court records. Roman Nose State Park area has local historical connections. The Watonga Public Library and Blaine County Historical Society have local cemetery and family records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial marriage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Blaine County; County Clerk deed books from 1890

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Bryan County formed 1907 at statehood from the Choctaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Durant. Named for William Jennings Bryan. Headquarters of the Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bryan County was part of the Choctaw Nation in Indian Territory. Durant serves as both the county seat and the headquarters of the Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma. Pre-statehood research should begin with Choctaw tribal records: Dawes Rolls (Choctaw by Blood, Choctaw Freedmen, Choctaw Mississippi), allotment records, and tribal court records at the Choctaw Nation and NARA Fort Worth. The county had a significant freedmen population -- Choctaw Freedmen rolls and Bureau of Indian Affairs records are essential. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory court records and newspapers. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). Southeastern Oklahoma State University in Durant has regional history collections. FamilySearch has indexed Oklahoma marriages and probate records. Bryan County Heritage Association has published cemetery transcriptions and family histories.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Bryan County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Caddo County formed 1901 after the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache (KCA) lands lottery. County seat is Anadarko. Named for the Caddo people. Multiple tribal communities in the county. Anadarko is called the 'Indian Capital of the Nation.'
county_metadata.research_tips: Caddo County was organized in 1901 after the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache (KCA) lands were opened to non-Indian settlement by lottery. The county is named for the Caddo people. NARA Fort Worth holds extensive records for the Kiowa, Comanche, and Apache tribes including agency records, allotment files, and annuity rolls. The Anadarko Agency of the Bureau of Indian Affairs administered multiple tribes in this area. Fort Sill (adjacent Comanche County) records are relevant. County Clerk records begin from 1901. BLM GLO land patents supplement county deed records. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial court records and newspapers. The Southern Plains Indian Museum in Anadarko has cultural and historical materials. FamilySearch has Oklahoma county marriage and probate records. Caddo County has diverse tribal heritage -- check multiple tribal enrollment offices for ancestors from different nations.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Caddo County; County Clerk deed books from 1901

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (1889 Land Run)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Canadian County formed 1890 as original Oklahoma Territory county from the 1889 Land Run. County seat is El Reno. Named for the Canadian River. Site of Fort Reno (est. 1874). Important railroad junction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Canadian County was one of the original Oklahoma Territory counties formed after the 1889 Land Run. The county seat, El Reno, was a major railroad junction and the location of the Cheyenne-Arapaho Agency. County Clerk records begin from 1889, making them among the oldest in Oklahoma. BLM GLO land patents document original claims from the Land Run. Fort Reno (est. 1874) records are relevant -- a military post that later served as a German/Italian POW camp in WWII. NARA Fort Worth holds Fort Reno records and Cheyenne-Arapaho Agency records. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds the extensive territorial newspaper collection for El Reno. Rock Island and Frisco railroad employment records may document workers. The Canadian County Historical Society and El Reno Carnegie Library have local collections. FamilySearch has Oklahoma Territory marriage records from the 1890s.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Canadian County; County Clerk deed books from 1890

source_profiles.source_name: Canadian County Deed Books / BLM GLO Patents -- County Clerk
source_profiles.repository: Canadian County Clerk, El Reno / Bureau of Land Management
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County deed books for Canadian County from 1890; BLM GLO federal land patents document original claims from the 1889 Land Run; County Clerk maintains deeds, mortgages, and liens
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1890; GLO patents are complete for federal land entries

source_profiles.source_name: Canadian County Probate Records -- District Court
source_profiles.repository: Canadian County District Court, El Reno
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships, and estate settlements for Canadian County; District Court has jurisdiction over probate in Oklahoma; guardianship records for minor allottees are particularly important in former Indian Territory counties

source_profiles.source_name: Canadian County Federal and Tribal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1890-1950 for Canadian County; Oklahoma Territory schedules; OHS holds territorial census records

source_profiles.source_name: Canadian County Land Run / Homestead Records -- BLM GLO / NARA / OHS
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management / NARA Fort Worth / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents (homestead, cash entry) for Canadian County from the 1889 Land Run; BLM GLO website has digitized patents; NARA Fort Worth holds Oklahoma Territory land office records including homestead applications and final proof files with affidavits and witness testimony; OHS has territorial census and newspaper records

source_profiles.source_name: Canadian County Marriage Records -- County Clerk / OHS / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Canadian County Clerk, El Reno / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and records for Canadian County from 1890; County Clerk issued marriage licenses in Oklahoma; OHS holds some county marriage records on microfilm; FamilySearch has indexed Oklahoma marriages; state vital records registration began 1908

source_profiles.source_name: Canadian County Cemetery Records -- Find A Grave / OKGenWeb
source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / OKGenWeb / Canadian County Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions for Canadian County including church cemeteries, municipal cemeteries, and pioneer burial grounds; Find A Grave has growing coverage; OKGenWeb and local historical societies have published transcriptions

county_metadata.record_losses: 1895 explosion and fire in Ardmore destroyed early Chickasaw Nation agency records; some county records lost in 1915 courthouse fire
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Carter County formed 1907 at statehood from the Chickasaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Ardmore. Named for Captain Ben W. Carter. Major oil-producing county. The 1895 railroad explosion and 1915 courthouse fire caused record losses.
county_metadata.research_tips: Carter County was part of the Chickasaw Nation in Indian Territory. The county seat, Ardmore, was a major commercial center before statehood. An 1895 railroad tank car explosion devastated downtown Ardmore and destroyed some early agency records. A 1915 courthouse fire caused additional record losses. Despite these losses, many records survive. Chickasaw Nation tribal records (Dawes Rolls, allotment records, citizenship files) are at the Chickasaw Nation headquarters in Ada and NARA Fort Worth. The Chickasaw Council House Museum in Tishomingo has historical materials. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). Carter County was a major oil-producing area -- oil lease records and royalty documents at the County Clerk office document families. The Greater Southwest Historical Museum in Ardmore has local history collections. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Carter County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

source_profiles.source_name: Carter County Land Records (post-loss) / Chickasaw Nation Allotment Records -- County Clerk / NARA
source_profiles.repository: Carter County Clerk, Ardmore / NARA Fort Worth
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County land records with gaps due to 1895 explosion and fire in Ardmore destroyed early Chickasaw Nation agency records; some county records lost in 1915 courthouse fire. Chickasaw Nation allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth supplement destroyed county deed books. County Clerk holds surviving and post-loss records from 1907
source_profiles.survival_notes: Incomplete: 1895 explosion and fire in Ardmore destroyed early Chickasaw Nation agency records; some county records lost in 1915 courthouse fire. Tribal allotment records at NARA provide critical backup

source_profiles.repository: Carter County District Court, Ardmore
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships, and estate settlements; District Court has jurisdiction over probate in Oklahoma; records incomplete due to 1895 explosion and fire in Ardmore destroyed early Chickasaw Nation agency records; some county records lost in 1915 courthouse fire
source_profiles.survival_notes: Incomplete: 1895 explosion and fire in Ardmore destroyed early Chickasaw Nation agency records; some county records lost in 1915 courthouse fire

source_profiles.source_name: Dawes Rolls -- Chickasaw Nation Enrollment Records / NARA
source_profiles.repository: NARA Fort Worth / Chickasaw Nation / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Dawes Commission enrollment records for Chickasaw citizens (Chickasaw by Blood, Chickasaw Freedmen) in Carter County area; includes application jackets with family information, census cards, and land allotment details; essential for tracing Chickasaw families before and after statehood

source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1900-1950 for Carter County; Chickasaw Nation census rolls and Indian Territory schedules at OHS and NARA

source_profiles.source_name: Carter County Oil Lease and Corporation Records -- County Clerk / Corporation Commission
source_profiles.repository: Carter County Clerk, Ardmore / Oklahoma Corporation Commission, Oklahoma City
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Oil and gas lease records, royalty agreements, well permits, and corporation filings for Carter County; County Clerk records oil/gas leases; Oklahoma Corporation Commission in Oklahoma City holds well records and production data; guardianship records for minor allottees with oil wealth are at the District Court

source_profiles.source_name: Carter County Indian Territory Court Records -- OHS / NARA
source_profiles.repository: Oklahoma Historical Society / NARA Fort Worth
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indian Territory court records for the Carter County area; US courts for Indian Territory (Northern, Central, Southern districts) handled criminal and civil cases; OHS holds court records, case files, and dockets; NARA Fort Worth has federal court records
